nativeRange.habitat: Shaded riverbanks, marshes, and wetlands in tropical West African rainforest; typically grows attached to wood and rock at the water margin and is frequently encountered emersed

decorPreferences: shaded areas to reduce leaf algae

goodWith: African cichlids (tough leaves resist most nibbling)

summary: A larger West African Anubias with arrow-shaped (spade-like) leaves on long stems. Generally considered beginner-friendly and slow-growing; the rhizome must be attached to hardscape, not buried.
careNotes: Anubias gracilis is one of the larger Anubias species, native to shaded riverbanks and marshes in Sierra Leone and Guinea (West Africa). It is immediately distinguished from the more common Anubias barteri by its arrowhead- or spade-shaped (triangular) leaves carried on noticeably long petioles, giving it an elegant, almost terrestrial silhouette. Despite the distinctive look, husbandry is the same as for other Anubias: it is a slow-growing epiphytic rhizome plant, and the rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge) must NOT be buried in substrate — burying it causes the plant to rot and melt. Instead, attach it to driftwood, rock, or other hardscape with thread, fishing line, or a dab of cyanoacrylate gel; the roots will eventually anchor on their own. Lighting is flexible: low to medium light suits it well, and stronger light is tolerated but tends to encourage algae on the slow-growing leaves; placement in shaded areas (under taller plants, behind hardscape, on the shaded side of wood) reduces leaf algae. CO2 is not required but is reported to improve growth rate and leaf quality when injected. Submerged it generally stays in the 15-40 cm range; grown emersed (in a paludarium or with leaves above the waterline) it can reach considerably larger sizes (sources report up to 50-60 cm). Cross-source size spread was relatively wide (Tropica 10-20+ cm, KFK 5-10 cm leaves on long stems, Aqua-fish.net 20-60 cm, AquariumLesson 30-60 cm, Fitz Fish Ponds 10-30 cm); the recorded 15-40 cm range represents a typical submerged adult plant. Temperature consensus across sources is roughly 22-28 °C, pH 5.5-7.8, gH 2-15 (Fitz Fish Ponds' lower-bound 12 °C is treated as a survival tolerance, not a recommended range). Less commonly seen in the trade than A. barteri and its cultivars; usually sold by specialist plant retailers (Tropica 101D, KFK, Buce Plant). Note that this species is sometimes confused with Anubias afzelii and Anubias heterophylla, which share the elongated-leaf habit.
breedingNotes: Propagated by rhizome division: with a sharp blade, cut a healthy rhizome into sections so each section retains several leaves and some attached roots. Re-attach each section to hardscape — do not bury. Like other Anubias, A. gracilis can flower underwater, producing the small white spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of the Araceae family, but seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed West African origin, slow growth, low light demand, low CO2 demand, easy difficulty, height 10-20+ cm submerged, triangular leaves 5-10 cm on long stems, and the critical rhizome-must-not-be-buried rule. Tropica search URL was used to find the direct product page (101D / id 17819).

additional.name: KeepFishKeeping
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirmed West African origin, triangular leaves of 5-10 cm on long leaf-stems, beginner-friendly difficulty, rhizome-must-not-be-buried rule, and pot-division propagation.

additional.name: Aqua-fish.net
additional.notes: Used for family (Araceae), refined native range (Sierra Leone and Guinea), temperature range (20-30 °C), pH range (5.6-8), gH range (5-18), and confirmation of moderate-to-low light and optional CO2.

additional.name: AquariumLesson
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Araceae), height range (30-60 cm including emersed growth), low light demand, slow growth, easy difficulty, temperature 22-26 °C, pH 5-7, and midground/background placement. Used to triangulate the wide adult-size spread across sources.

additional.name: Fitz Fish Ponds
additional.notes: Cross-checked submerged size (10-30 cm), low-to-medium light, low CO2, slow growth, origin (Guinea, Sierra Leone), and midground/background/epiphyte placement options.
